input_statement: Most of the ideas in this document are known to experienced developers.

input_statement: MANAGEMENT PRINCIPLES
There are many models of development management, of which different variations of Agile are now prevailing.
Moreover, this methodology has acquired the status of dogma and has expectedly spawned sects of fanatics.
C: has acquired the status of dogma and has spawned sects of fanatics

input_statement: We do not follow any ready-made model and use our own, taking the best of different approaches.
C: and use our own, taking the best of different approaches

input_statement: At the same time, we avoid dogmatism and the temptation to "bronze".
C: at the same time, avoiding dogmatism and the temptation to 'bronze'

input_statement: What we take from Agile is flexibility.
This means that we change any interfering rules.
C: that

input_statement: Iterativity and related rituals are not necessary because of the specifics of our software consumers.
C: because of the specifics of our software consumers

input_statement: We keep the lack of hierarchy in anarchic self-organization:
- direct communication of everyone with everyone on any issue
- direct communication between developer and customer
- network organization of working groups
- multiple roles of a participant depending on his involvement in a particular project.
(technical leader in project A, executor in project B, customer in project C)
From free software development techniques we take the version control system and the bug accounting system.
C: from free software development techniques

input_statement: These systems were widely used in the early 90's in the framework of Mozilla project and other free software development,
although for some reason they are considered to be attributes of Agile.
C: although for some reason they are considered to be attributes of Agile

input_statement: From the bureaucracy of project development we take historicity, the ability to find something that has already been there once.
C: from the bureaucracy of project development

input_statement: Remember: LIFE is the self-organization of chaotic matter.
Chaos is characterized by self-organization on energy flows.
C: self-organization on energy flows

input_statement: We are this CHAOS.
LIFE itself will correct us when we begin to slouch (not some magic techniques).
CODING PRINCIPLES
In coding style, the main thing is names.
Not brackets and indents.
The ability to name, to name, is an indicator of the developer's level.
If you give a good and appropriate name to a variable or function or class, everything else will almost automatically be good.
I: give a good and appropriate name
C: if you give a good and appropriate name

input_statement: If the opposite is true, then almost automatically everything else will be bad.
C: if the opposite is true

input_statement: A good name doesn't mean a long name.
It means appropriate, suitable, apt, accurately reflecting the essence, best characterized,
grammatically correct, usable and recognizable by others in a similar context.
You may have noticed that in Visual Studio's Refactoring submenu, the Rename item comes first.
A wrong choice will sprout in the code forever!
Be afraid of misprints like imfo or autometic - you make a mistake once and for all.
Worst of all, if such a misprinted identifier gets into the interface!!!
If you have code spread across several subsystems in different programming languages and interfaces, use the same name everywhere.
C: everywhere

input_statement: It's hard to look for inconsistencies when a variable sounds like errno in C++, is passed to HTTP API as errLvl,
and in the logs it is written as errorLevel.
C: in the logs, as errorLevel

input_statement: * Comments explain what well-chosen names don't.
C: what comments do

input_statement: We have a lot of magic being used.
Explain _intentions_ other than _methods_, as much as possible, with extended comments.
C: as much as possible, with extended comments

input_statement: One should write not only "what I do and how I do it", but primarily "why I do it, what goal I achieve, what problem I solve".
B: what I do and how I do it, why I do it, what goal I achieve, what problem I solve

good

In compiled programming languages (hereinafter referred to as PHP) comments are written in Russian.

input_statement: In interpreted languages, comments and identifiers are in English only, only grammatically clean!
C: in interpreted languages, only grammatically clean

input_statement: Scripts that have fallen to the enemy must not indicate your nationality.
* The best code is the one that doesn't exist.
Don't write too much.
Don't make plans for years to come.
"Tomorrow may never come, and no one will need it.
"Tomorrow" will turn out to be wrong.
C: tomorrow

input_statement: Brevity, minimalism is everything.
Don't create entities.
That's the greatest sin of an engineer!
Cut off all unnecessary things with Occam's Razor - frameworks, OOP, third-party libraries, wrappers.
C: with Occam's Razor - frameworks, OOP, third-party libraries, wrappers

input_statement: Ideal code should occupy zero bytes (this is a real case when using someone else's software).
* there is no sense in writing code if it has already been written by someone else.
Before writing it, search the Web.

input_statement: If you have taken someone else's code, be sure to put a link to the source code where it was taken from.
C: where it was taken from

input_statement: ERROR HANDLING
We suggest the following method of error handling in code:
B: the following method of error handling in code

input_statement: - C++ exceptions are not used
- SEH/VEH exceptions are used only for removing the postmortem stack or for protecting dangerous code sections (e.g. execution of arbitrary shell code).
C: only for removing the postmortem stack or for protecting dangerous code sections (e.g. execution of arbitrary shell code)

input_statement: (for example, execution of arbitrary shell code)
- all functions write about the error immediately in stderr, preceding the line with the function name.
C: preceding the line with the function name

input_statement: For example:
fprintf(stderr, __FUNCTION__
" Invalid file handle\r\n");
- if the function returns a collection (for example, of found characters), then in case of an error the return is an empty collection;
C: if the function returns a collection (for example, of found characters), then in case of an error

input_statement: - if the function returns a pointer, then NULL is an error;
- if the function returns a string, an empty string is an error;
- if the function returns an integer, 0 or -1 is an error;
The calling context checks the return, and if it is unexpected and there is no way to substitute a default value,
C: if it is unexpected and there is no way to substitute a default value

input_statement: we make the return from the current location according to the above error signaling rules.
C: according to the above error signaling rules

input_statement: GIT POLICY
* We use Git not because everyone else does, but to accomplish these tasks:
1. Backup.
We've seen many cases of work lost over long periods of time (months, and once a year), simply due to developer sloppiness.
Multiple people working together on a project
3. Finding the author of specific lines of code
4. Finding a regression by rolling back and successive builds through history
C: by rolling back and successive builds through history

input_statement: Maintaining the history of changes (Release notes, Changelog)
C: (Release notes, Changelog)

input_statement: Versioning, release management
That's why the rules below are written for these benefits.
C: for these benefits

input_statement: There may be other rules for other tasks.
* Commits are mandatory whenever there is something to commit (that is, just about every day).
C: whenever there is something to commit

input_statement: Even if the code is down and not finished, commit to a temporary branch.
C: to a temporary branch

input_statement: It's better than losing a day's work.
* It is desirable to keep stable code in a separate branch, and inject only checked changes into it.
C: in a separate branch

input_statement: * If a project has versions, each version corresponds to either a branch (if it is passed for support),
or a label (if only historical without support).
* If you are alone on the project, you maintain branches as you like.
C: as you like

input_statement: * If more than one person is on the project, then development should be done in a private branch.
C: in a private branch

input_statement: Merging into the main branch is always done by a double merge.
- first from the main branch to your branch (with conflict resolution and smoke test).
- then from its own branch to the main branch.
A commit should always have a comment, in Russian, describing the brief essence of the changes.
C: in Russian, describing the brief essence of the changes

input_statement: For example: "Fix network buffer overflow on high frequency of requests".
C: on high frequency of requests

input_statement: The comment should include the number of the bug or task from the accounting system,
C: the number of the bug or task from the accounting system

input_statement: For example: "B-1655 Fix network buffer overflow on high request frequency".
When you put the bug ID, Git's integration with the bug accounting system will automatically associate the commit with the bug.
C: when you put the bug ID, and Git's integration with the bug accounting system is active

input_statement: * Commits should be atomic and minimal.
One commit is one bug/task.
This means that you can no longer fug a git push without figuring it out directly from the project directory.
C: without figuring it out directly from the project directory

input_statement: If you are command line, you should list the files by name and make sure to diff with upstream before committing.
C: by name, with upstream

input_statement: Only those files that are related to a single task are commited.
C: that are related to a single task

input_statement: Only those lines of a file that are relevant to a single task are committed.
C: that are relevant to a single task

input_statement: If this rule is not followed, the following benefits of Git become unavailable or difficult to achieve:
C: if this rule is not followed
O: become unavailable or difficult to achieve

input_statement: - cherry pick (moving code between non-synchronous branches)
- blame (finding the author of the code) becomes very noisy
- searching for regressions by successive builds through history (when an error occurs in such a commit, it is impossible to tell which change it refers to)
* Use UI utilities to work with Git.
They show you exactly what has been changed relative to the main branch,
C: relative to the main branch

input_statement: allow you to send only individual lines from the file, and ignore your personal debugging changes and customizations,
C: and ignore your personal debugging changes and customizations

input_statement: that shouldn't make it to the server.
* It is forbidden to commit
- any texts with real names, nicknames, passwords, URLs and IP addresses of our resources, addresses of cryptocurrencies, etc., etc. (unless specifically stated).
- archives and binary files that are not part of the project build steps
BUG ACCOUNTING AND PROJECT DEVELOPMENT SYSTEM
We use this system not because everyone uses them, but for the following tasks:
To reduce writing.
You can poke at a text that has already been written, instead of writing to someone repeatedly.
B: a text that has already been written

input_statement: It can be a task text, a comment describing the steps of reproducing an error, a picture or a log in an attachment.
To find the ends (historicity).
Errors can be repeated, especially floating errors.
C: especially floating errors

input_statement: A large number of errors are grouped around the same code lines.
In short, the situation "this has already happened" is frequent.
For knowledge concentration and analytics.
After some time a considerable amount of information is accumulated in the system.
C: after some time, a considerable amount is accumulated in the system

input_statement: Where should a developer or manager go for information, but not to this system?
For development management.
Maintaining a list of tasks, assigning tasks to executors, prioritizing, tracking work stages, scheduling.
Fellow developers, when there are a hundred of you and I am one (well, two or three), it is very hard to keep it all in my head.
A bug tracker is, among other things, your work log.
C: your work log

input_statement: When working on a task, it is normal to write down notes for yourself.

input_statement: When something works or something doesn't work - notes like "this didn't work, here's the problem formulation" are very valuable.
